volumeTitle: Birds. Lysistrata. Women at the Thesmophoria
label: Birds (Section 671 - Section 826)

sections.label: Section 671
sections.original: ἐγὼ μὲν αὐτὴν κἂν φιλῆσαί μοι δοκῶ .
sections.translation: Oh ! how I should like to kiss her !

sections.label: Section 672
sections.original: ἀλλʼ ὦ κακόδαιμον ῥύγχος ὀβελίσκοιν ἔχει .
sections.translation: Why , wretched man , she has two little sharp points on her beak !

sections.label: Section 673
sections.original: ἀλλʼ ὥσπερ ᾠὸν νὴ Δίʼ ἀπολέψαντα χρὴ
sections.translation: I would treat her like an egg , the shell of which we remove before eating it ; I would take off her mask and then kiss her pretty face .

sections.label: Section 675
sections.original: ἴωμεν .
sections.translation: Let us go in .

sections.label: Section 675b
sections.original: ἡγοῦ δὴ σὺ νῷν τύχἀγαθῇ .
sections.translation: Lead the way , and may success attend us . Epops goes into the thicket , followed by Pisthetaerus and Euelpides .

sections.label: Section 676
sections.original: ὦ φίλη , ὦ ξουθή ,
sections.translation: Lovable golden bird , whom I cherish above all others , you , whom I associate with all my songs , nightingale ,

sections.label: Section 680
sections.original: ἦλθες ἦλθες ὤφθης ,
sections.translation: you have come , you have come , to show yourself to me and to charm me with your notes . Come , you , who play spring melodies upon the harmonious flute , lead off our anapests .

sections.label: Section 685
sections.original: ἄγε δὴ φύσιν ἄνδρες ἀμαυρόβιοι , φύλλων γενεᾷ προσόμοιοι ,
sections.translation: Weak mortals , chained to the earth , creatures of clay as frail as the foliage of the woods , you unfortunate race , whose life is but darkness , as unreal as a shadow , the illusion of a dream , hearken to us , who are immortal beings , ethereal , ever young and occupied with eternal thoughts , for we shall teach you about all celestial matters ;

sections.label: Section 690
sections.original: ἵνʼ ἀκούσαντες πάντα παρʼ ἡμῶν ὀρθῶς περὶ τῶν μετεώρων .
sections.translation: you shall know thoroughly what is the nature of the birds , what the origin of the gods , of the rivers , of Erebus , and Chaos ; thanks to us , even Prodicus will envy you your knowledge .

sections.label: Section 693
sections.original: Χάος ἦν καὶ Νὺξ Ἔρεβός τε μέλαν πρῶτον καὶ Τάρταρος εὐρύς ,
sections.translation: At the beginning there was only Chaos , Night , dark Erebus , and deep Tartarus . Earth , the air and heaven had no existence .

sections.label: Section 695
sections.original: τίκτει πρώτιστον ὑπηνέμιον Νὺξ ἡ μελανόπτερος ᾠόν ,
sections.translation: Firstly , blackwinged Night laid a germless egg in the bosom of the infinite deeps of Erebus , and from this , after the revolution of long ages , sprang the graceful Eros with his glittering golden wings , swift as the whirlwinds of the tempest . He mated in deep Tartarus with dark Chaos , winged like himself , and thus hatched forth our race , which was the first to see the light .

sections.label: Section 700
sections.original: πρότερον δʼ οὐκ ἦν γένος ἀθανάτων , πρὶν Ἔρως ξυνέμειξεν ἅπαντα ·
sections.translation: That of the Immortals did not exist until Eros had brought together all the ingredients of the world , and from their marriage Heaven , Ocean , Earth and the imperishable race of blessed gods sprang into being . Thus our origin is very much older than that of the dwellers in Olympus . We are the offspring of Eros ; there are a thousand proofs to show it . We have wings and we lend assistance to lovers .

sections.label: Section 705
sections.original: πολλοὺς δὲ καλοὺς ἀπομωμοκότας παῖδας πρὸς τέρμασιν ὥρας
sections.translation: How many handsome youths , who had sworn to remain insensible , have opened their thighs because of our power and have yielded themselves to their lovers when almost at the end of their youth , being led away by the gift of a quail , a waterfowl , a goose , or a cock .

sections.label: Section 708
sections.original: πάντα δὲ θνητοῖς ἐστὶν ἀφʼ ἡμῶν τῶν ὀρνίθων τὰ μέγιστα .
sections.translation: And what important services do not the birds render to mortals ! First of all , they mark the seasons for them , springtime , winter , and autumn .

sections.label: Section 710
sections.original: σπείρειν μέν , ὅταν γέρανος κρώζουσʼ ἐς τὴν Λιβύην μεταχωρῇ .
sections.translation: Does the screaming crane migrate to Libya , — it warns the husbandman to sow , the pilot to take his ease beside his tiller hung up in his dwelling , and Orestes to weave a tunic , so that the rigorous cold may not drive him any more to strip other folk . When the kite reappears , he tells of the return of spring and of the period when the fleece of the sheep must be clipped . Is the swallow in sight ?

sections.label: Section 715
sections.original: ὅτε χρὴ χλαῖναν πωλεῖν ἤδη καὶ ληδάριόν τι πρίασθαι .
sections.translation: All hasten to sell their warm tunic and to buy some light clothing . We are your Ammon , Delphi , Dodona , your Phoebus Apollo . Before undertaking anything , whether a business transaction , a marriage , or the purchase of food , you consult the birds by reading the omens , and you give this name of omen to all signs that tell of the future .

sections.label: Section 720
sections.original: φήμη γʼ ὑμῖν ὄρνις ἐστί , πταρμόν τʼ ὄρνιθα καλεῖτε ,
sections.translation: With you a word is an omen , you call a sneeze an omen , a meeting an omen , an unknown sound an omen , a slave or an ass an omen . Is it not clear that we are a prophetic Apollo to you ?

sections.label: Section 723
sections.original: ἢν οὖν ἡμᾶς νομίσητε θεούς ,
sections.translation: If you recognize us as gods , we shall be your divining Muses , through us you will know the winds and the seasons , summer ,

sections.label: Section 725
sections.original: αὔραις ὥραις χειμῶνι θέρει
sections.translation: winter , and the temperate months . We shall not withdraw ourselves to the highest clouds like Zeus , but shall be among you and shall give to you

sections.label: Section 730
sections.original: αὐτοῖς , παισίν , παίδων παισίν ,
sections.translation: and to your children and the children of your children , health and wealth , long life , peace , youth , laughter , songs and feasts ; in short , you will all be so well off ,

sections.label: Section 735
sections.original: κοπιᾶν ὑμῖν ὑπὸ τῶν ἀγαθῶν ·
sections.translation: that you will be weary and cloyed with enjoyment .

sections.label: Section 1.737
sections.original: Μοῦσα λοχμαία ,
sections.translation: Oh , rustic Muse of such varied note , tiotiotiotiotiotinx , I sing with you

sections.label: Section 1.740
sections.original: νάπαισι καὶ κορυφαῖς ἐν ὀρείαις ,
sections.translation: in the groves and on the mountain tops , tiotiotiotinx . I poured forth sacred strains from my golden throat

sections.label: Section 1.745
sections.original: Πανὶ νόμους ἱεροὺς ἀναφαίνω
sections.translation: in honor of the god Pan , tiotiotiotinx , from the top of the thickly leaved ash , and my voice mingles with the mighty choirs who extol Cybele on the mountain tops , totototototototototinx . ' Tis to our concerts that Phrynichus comes to pillage like a bee the ambrosia of his songs ,

sections.label: Section 1.750
sections.original: φέρων γλυκεῖαν ᾠδάν .
sections.translation: the sweetness of which so charms the ear , tiotiotiotinx .

sections.label: Section 752
sections.original: εἰ μετʼ ὀρνίθων τις ὑμῶν ὦ θεαταὶ βούλεται
sections.translation: If there is one of you spectators who wishes to spend the rest of his life quietly among the birds , let him come to us .

sections.label: Section 755
sections.original: ὅσα γάρ ἐστιν ἐνθάδʼ αἰσχρὰ τῷ νόμῳ κρατούμενα ,
sections.translation: All that is disgraceful and forbidden by law on earth is on the contrary honorable among us , the birds . For instance , among you it ' s a crime to beat your father , but with us it ' s an estimable deed ; it ' s considered fine to run straight at your father and hit him , saying , Come , lift your spur if you want to fight .

sections.label: Section 760
sections.original: εἰ δὲ τυγχάνει τις ὑμῶν δραπέτης ἐστιγμένος ,
sections.translation: The runaway slave , whom you brand , is only a spotted francolin with us . Are you Phrygian like Spintharus ? Among us you would be the Phrygian bird , the goldfinch , of the race of Philemon . Are you a slave and a Carian like Execestides ?

sections.label: Section 765
sections.original: φυσάτω πάππους παρʼ ἡμῖν , καὶ φανοῦνται φράτερες .
sections.translation: Among us you can create yourself forefathers ; you can always find relations . Does the son of Pisias want to betray the gates of the city to the foe ? Let him become a partridge , the fitting offspring of his father ; among us there is no shame in escaping as cleverly as a partridge .

sections.label: Section 769
sections.original: τοιάδε κύκνοι ,
sections.translation: So the swans on the banks of the Hebrus ,

sections.label: Section 770
sections.original: τιὸ τιὸ τιὰ τιὸ τιὸ τιοτίγξ ,
sections.translation: tiotiotiotiotiotinx , mingle their voices to serenade Apollo , tiotiotiotinx , flapping their wings the while ,

sections.label: Section 775
sections.original: τιὸ τιὸ τιὸ τιοτίγξ ,
sections.translation: tiotiotiotinx ; their notes reach beyond the clouds of heaven ; they startle the various tribes of the beasts ; a windless sky calms the waves , totototototototototinx ;

sections.label: Section 780
sections.original: πᾶς δʼ ἐπεκτύπησʼ Ὄλυμπος ·
sections.translation: all Olympus resounds , and astonishment seizes its rulers ; the Olympian graces and Muses cry aloud the strain , tiotiotiotinx .

sections.label: Section 785
sections.original: οὐδέν ἐστʼ ἄμεινον οὐδʼ ἥδιον ἢ φῦσαι πτερά .
sections.translation: There is nothing more useful nor more pleasant than to have wings . To begin with , just let us suppose a spectator to be dying with hunger and to be weary of the choruses of the tragic poets ; if he were winged , he would fly off , go home to dine and come back with his stomach filled .

sections.label: Section 790
sections.original: εἴ τε Πατροκλείδης τις ὑμῶν τυγχάνει χεζητιῶν ,
sections.translation: Some Patroclides , needing to take a crap , would not have to spill it out on his cloak , but could fly off , satisfy his requirements , let a few farts and , having recovered his breath , return . If one of you , it matters not who , had adulterous relations and saw the husband of his mistress in the seats of the senators ,

sections.label: Section 795
sections.original: οὗτος ἂν πάλιν παρʼ ὑμῶν πτερυγίσας ἀνέπτετο ,
sections.translation: he might stretch his wings , fly to her , and , having laid her , resume his place . Is it not the most priceless gift of all , to be winged ? Look at Diitrephes ! His wings were only wicker-work ones , and yet he got himself chosen Phylarch and then Hipparch ; from being nobody ,

sections.label: Section 800
sections.original: μεγάλα πράττει κἀστὶ νυνὶ ξουθὸς ἱππαλεκτρυών .
sections.translation: he has risen to be famous ; he ' s now the finest gilded cock of his tribe .

sections.label: Section 801
sections.original: ταυτὶ τοιαυτί · μὰ Δίʼ ἐγὼ μὲν πρᾶγμά πω
sections.translation: Halloa ! What ' s this ? By Zeus ! I never saw anything so funny in all my life .

sections.label: Section 803
sections.original: ἐπὶ τῷ γελᾷς ;
sections.translation: What makes you laugh ?

sections.label: Section 803b
sections.original: ἐπὶ τοῖσι σοῖς ὠκυπτέροις .
sections.translation: Your little wings . D ' you know what you look like ?

sections.label: Section 805
sections.original: εἰς εὐτέλειαν χηνὶ συγγεγραμμένῳ .
sections.translation: Like a goose painted by some dauber .

sections.label: Section 806
sections.original: σὺ δὲ κοψίχῳ γε σκάφιον ἀποτετιλμένῳ .
sections.translation: And you look like a close-shaven blackbird .

sections.label: Section 807
sections.original: ταυτὶ μὲν ᾐκάσμεσθα κατὰ τὸν Αἰσχύλον ·
sections.translation: We ourselves asked for this transformation , and , as Aeschylus has it , These are no borrowed feathers , but truly our own .

sections.label: Section 809
sections.original: ἄγε δὴ τί χρὴ δρᾶν ;
sections.translation: Come now , what must be done ?

sections.label: Section 809b
sections.original: πρῶτον ὄνομα τῇ πόλει
sections.translation: First give our city

sections.label: Section 810
sections.original: θέσθαι τι μέγα καὶ κλεινόν , εἶτα τοῖς θεοῖς
sections.translation: a great and famous name , then sacrifice to the gods .

sections.label: Section 811b
sections.original: ταῦτα κἀμοὶ συνδοκεῖ .
sections.translation: I think so too .

sections.label: Section 812
sections.original: φέρʼ ἴδω , τί δʼ ἡμῖν τοὔνομʼ ἔσται τῇ πόλει ;
sections.translation: Let ' s see . What shall our city be called ?

sections.label: Section 813
sections.original: βούλεσθε τὸ μέγα τοῦτο τοὐκ Λακεδαίμονος
sections.translation: Will you have a high-sounding Laconian name ? Shall we call it Sparta ?

sections.label: Section 814b
sections.original: Ἡράκλεις ·
sections.translation: What !

sections.label: Section 815
sections.original: Σπάρτην γὰρ ἂν θείμην ἐγὼ τἠμῇ πόλει ;
sections.translation: call my town Sparta ? Why , I would not use esparto for my bed , even though I had nothing but bands of rushes .

sections.label: Section 817
sections.original: τί δῆτʼ ὄνομʼ αὐτῇ θησόμεσθʼ ;
sections.translation: Well then , what name can you suggest ?

sections.label: Section 817b
sections.original: ἐντευθενὶ
sections.translation: Some name borrowed from the clouds , from these lofty regions in which we dwell — in short , some well-known name .

sections.label: Section 819b
sections.original: βούλει Νεφελοκοκκυγίαν ;
sections.translation: Do you like Nephelococcygia ?

sections.label: Section 820
sections.original: ἰοὺ ἰού ·
sections.translation: Oh ! capital !

sections.label: Section 820a
sections.original: καλόν γʼ ἀτεχνῶς σὺ καὶ μέγʼ ηὗρες τοὔνομα .
sections.translation: truly that ' s a brilliant thought !

sections.label: Section 821
sections.original: ἆρʼ ἐστὶν αὑτηγὶ Νεφελοκοκκυγία ,
sections.translation: Is it in Nephelococcygia that all the wealth of Theogenes and most of Aeschines ' is ?

sections.label: Section 823
sections.original: καὶ λῷστον μὲν οὖν
sections.translation: No , it ' s rather the plain of Phlegra , where the gods

sections.label: Section 825
sections.original: ἀλαζονευόμενοι καθυπερηκόντισαν .
sections.translation: withered the pride of the sons of the Earth with their shafts .

sections.label: Section 826
sections.original: λιπαρὸν τὸ χρῆμα τῆς πόλεως . τίς δαὶ θεὸς
sections.translation: Oh ! what a splendid city ! But what god shall be its patron ? for whom shall we weave the peplus ?
